                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Corpus Christi TX",
                "headline": "Freeze Warning issued January 29 at 3:01AM CST until January 29 at 8:00AM CST by NWS Corpus Christi TX",
                "description": "* WHAT...Sub-freezing temperatures as low as 30.\n\n* WHERE...Goliad and Victoria Counties.\n\n* WHEN...Until 8 AM CST this morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...Frost and freeze conditions could kill crops, other\nsensitive vegetation and possibly damage unprotected outdoor\nplumbing.",
                "instruction": "Take steps now to protect tender plants from the cold.",
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {

                "event": "Winter Storm Watch",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Chicago IL",
                "headline": "Winter Storm Watch issued January 29 at 2:58AM CST until January 31 at 6:00PM CST by NWS Chicago IL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy lake effect snow with total snow accumulations over 6\ninches possible. The heaviest snow is expected to impact areas\nnear and north of US Route 30.\n\n* WHERE...Lake IN and Porter Counties.\n\n* WHEN...From Friday afternoon through Saturday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult. The hazardous conditions\ncould impact the Friday evening commute.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...An initial period of accumulating snow is\nexpected Friday morning. However, the main window for the heaviest\nsnowfall is expected Friday evening through Saturday morning. Some\nbreaks in the snowfall are possible at times as the snow band may\nwobble Friday night and Saturday.",
                "instruction": "Monitor the latest forecasts for updates on this situation.\n\nDuring lake effect snow, the weather can vary from bands of locally\nheavy snow to dry weather just a few miles away. Visibilities can\nalso vary greatly. Be prepared for rapid changes in weather,\nvisibility, and road conditions.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "WSWLOT"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWUS43 KLOT 290858"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [

                    ],

For the Storm\nWatch, north winds 35 to 40 kt with gusts up to 55 kt and\nwaves 1 to 3 ft possible.\n\n* WHERE...Currituck Sound.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 10 AM EST this\nmorning. For the Storm Watch, from Saturday afternoon through\nSunday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Storm force winds and hazardous waves could capsize\nor damage vessels and reduce visibility.",

For the Gale Watch, north winds 25\nto 30 kt with gusts up to 45 kt and waves 2 to 3 ft possible.\n\n* WHERE...Rappahannock River from Urbanna to Windmill Point,\nYork River and James River from Jamestown to the James River\nBridge.\n\n* WHEN...From Saturday evening through Sunday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Strong winds can cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",

                "event": "Small Craft Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Wakefield VA",
                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 29 at 3:53AM EST until January 30 at 4:00AM EST by NWS Wakefield VA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 15 to 20\nkt with gusts up to 25 kt and waves 2 to 4 ft. For the\nFreezing Spray Advisory, moderate accumulation of freezing\nspray expected. For the Storm Watch, north winds 35 to 40 kt\nwith gusts up to 50 kt and waves 5 to 8 ft possible.\n\n* WHERE...Chesapeake Bay from Smith Point to Windmill Point VA.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 4 AM EST Friday.\nFor the Freezing Spray Advisory, until 4 PM EST this\nafternoon. For the Storm Watch, from Saturday evening through\nSunday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in freezing spray is hazardous.\nFreezing spray may render mechanical and electronic components\ninoperative. Ice accretion on decks and superstructures may\nresult in some loss of stability. Storm force winds and\nhazardous waves could capsize or damage vessels and reduce\nvisibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for ice accumulation on their vessel and\nconsider altering plans to avoid or mitigate these hazardous\nconditions.\n\nMariners should prepare to remain in port, alter course, and/or\nsecure the vessel before the onset of severe conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {

                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [

                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 29 at 3:27AM EST until January 29 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Marquette MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds around 15\nkt with gusts up to 20 kt and waves 3 to 5 ft occurring. For\nthe Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a\nrate of 2 cm per hour or greater expected, and may rapidly\naccumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Eagle River to Manitou Island MI.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 7 AM EST this\nmorning. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 7 PM this\nevening to 7 PM EST Friday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of stability.\nConditions will be hazardous to small craft.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel.                 "description": "* WHAT...Snow.                 "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued January 29 at 3:23AM EST until January 29 at 1:00PM EST by NWS Grand Rapids MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...Snow. Additional snow accumulations up to one inch.\n\n* WHERE...Kalamazoo County.\n\n* WHEN...Until 1 PM EST this afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Roads, and especially bridges and overpasses, will\nlikely become slick and hazardous. Visibility and road conditions\nmay change rapidly over short distances in lake effect snow. Plan\non slippery road conditions. The hazardous conditions will likely\nimpact the Thursday morning commute.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Lake effect snow continues near the lakeshore\nthis morning. An increase in intensity and aerial coverage is\nexpected this morning as snow bands work down the long axis of the\nlake. Some of these snow bands will work into Kalamazoo County\nresulting in periods of heavier lake effect snow.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Northern Indiana",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 29 at 3:22AM EST until January 29 at 9:00AM EST by NWS Northern Indiana",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 15 below zero.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of northern Indiana and northwest Ohio.\n\n* WHEN...Until 9 AM EST this morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 15 below zero could\nresult in hypothermia or frostbite if precautions are not taken.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "event": "Rip Current Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Juan PR",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued January 29 at 3:54AM AST until January 29 at 6:00PM AST by NWS San Juan PR",
                "description": "* WHAT...High Rip Current Risk, life-threatening rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...Northwest to northeast beaches of Puerto Rico, including\nCulebra. St. Thomas in the U.S. Virgin Islands.\n\n* WHEN...From this morning through late this afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water, where it becomes difficult to\nreturn to safety.",
                "instruction": "There is a high risk of rip currents.\n\nRip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as groins, jetties\nand piers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nIf you become caught in a rip current, yell for help. Remain\ncalm, do not exhaust yourself and stay afloat while waiting for\nhelp. If you have to swim out of a rip current, swim parallel to\nshore and back toward the beach when possible. Do not attempt to\nswim directly against a rip current as you will tire quickly.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWSJU"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHCA42 TJSJ 290754"
                    ],

                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow possible. Total snow accumulations between 5 and\n8 inches possible. Locally significant snowfall totals will also\nbe possible.                 "description": "* WHAT...Dangerously cold wind chills as low as 6 above possible.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of south central and southwest Alabama, northwest\nFlorida, and southeast Mississippi.\n\n* WHEN...From late Friday night through Sunday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...Frostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin\nis exposed to these temperatures.                 "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake sure outdoor animals have a warm, dry shelter, food, and\nunfrozen water.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 15 to 20 kt\nwith gusts up to 25 kt and choppy waters expected.                 "headline": "Winter Storm Watch issued January 29 at 2:16AM EST until February 1 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Greenville-Spartanburg SC",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow possible. Total snow accumulations between 4 and\n7 inches east of I-26. Total accumulations between 2 and 4 inches\nacross the remainder of the area. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Northeast Georgia, western North Carolina, and Upstate\nSouth Carolina.\n\n* WHEN...From Friday afternoon through Sunday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...The heavy snow will make many roads impassable and may\nproduce scattered power outages due to the weight of the snow on\ntree limbs and power lines. The hazardous conditions could impact\nthe Friday evening commute. Gusty winds could bring down tree\nbranches.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Snow is expected to rapidly stick to roads\nand other surfaces due to temperatures in the 20s. Travel\nconditions could deteriorate rapidly as early as Friday evening.\nVery gusty winds on Saturday could result in areas of blowing\nsnow, causing very poor visibility.",
                "instruction": "Monitor the latest forecasts for updates on this situation.",

                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 10 below.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of central, northern, and southern Delaware,\nnortheast Maryland, central, northern, northwest, and southern New\nJersey, and southeast Pennsylvania.\n\n* WHEN...Until 10 AM EST Friday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 10 below zero could\nresult in hypothermia if precautions are not taken.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 15 to 25 kt\nwith gusts up to 30 kt and seas 3 to 5 ft expected.                 "instruction": "During freezing spray conditions the U.S. Coast Guard advises\nthat you ensure all lifesaving equipment remains free of ice.\n\nMariners should prepare for accumulation of ice on their vessel\nand consider altering plans to avoid or mitigate these hazardous\nnavigating conditions.\n\nInexperienced mariners, especially those operating smaller\nvessels, should avoid navigating in hazardous conditions.",

                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 29 at 1:08AM EST until January 30 at 10:00AM EST by NWS Buffalo NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...Bitterly cold wind chills as low as 24 below zero expected.\n\n* WHERE...All of Western New York.                 "instruction": "This is a potentially hazardous situation to be outdoors. If you\nmust be outside, be sure to cover all exposed skin. Frostbite can\noccur in 30 minutes or less with apparent temperatures of 15 below\nzero or colder.",

                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued January 29 at 1:03AM EST until January 31 at 10:00AM EST by NWS Buffalo NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...Lake effect snow expected. Total snow accumulations 6 to 12\ninches in the most persistent snowbands.\n\n* WHERE...Orleans, Monroe, and Wayne Counties. Greatest snowfall\namounts will be closer to the Lake Ontario shoreline and across\neastern Monroe to Wayne County.\n\n* WHEN...From 10 AM this morning to 10 AM EST Saturday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel will be very difficult. The hazardous conditions\nwill impact the Thursday evening and Friday morning commutes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A fluffy snow is expected, with 12- hour\nperiod to period amounts of several inches adding up to 6 to 12\ninches over the course of the advisory.",
                "instruction": "Lake effect snow will fall in relatively narrow bands. If traveling,\nbe prepared for rapidly changing road conditions and visibilities.\n\nSubmit snow reports through our website or social media.\n\nDuring lake effect snow, the weather can vary from bands of locally\nheavy snow with greatly reduced visibilities to dry conditions just\na few miles away.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 15 to 25\nkt with gusts up to 30 kt and seas 1 to 4 ft expected.                 "instruction": "Mariners should consider altering plans to avoid or mitigate\nhazardous accumulation of ice on their vessel.                 "event": "Extreme Cold Watch",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Jackson MS",
                "headline": "Extreme Cold Watch issued January 28 at 11:57PM CST until January 31 at 6:00PM CST by NWS Jackson MS",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerously cold wind chills from as low as -2 degrees\nnorth to as low as 5 degrees above in the south are possible.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of southeast Arkansas, northeast Louisiana, and\ncentral, east central, north central, northeast, northwest, south\ncentral, southeast, southwest, and west central Mississippi.\n\n* WHEN...From late Friday night through Saturday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Frostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin\nis exposed to these temperatures. An extended period of freezing\ntemperatures could cause ruptured water pipes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Strong gusty winds of 30 to 40 mph will\nresult in even lower wind chills at times.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nTo prevent water pipes from freezing; wrap or drain or allow them to\ndrip slowly.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake sure outdoor animals have a warm, dry shelter, food, and\nunfrozen water.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.\n\nMonitor the latest forecasts and warnings for updates.",
                "response": "Monitor",
                "parameters": {

                        "NPWJAN"
                    ],

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Birmingham AL",
                "headline": "Extreme Cold Watch issued January 28 at 11:24PM CST until February 1 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Birmingham AL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerously cold wind chills ranging from 3 below zero to\nthe single digits above zero are forecast.\n\n* WHERE...All of Central Alabama.\n\n* WHEN...From late Friday night through Sunday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...Frostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin\nis exposed to these temperatures. An extended period of\nsubfreezing temperatures could cause pipes to burst.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.\n\nIf you do not have heat in your home or lack shelter, you can call\n211 or visit 211connectsalabama.org for assistance locating\nappropriate shelter from the cold.\n\nTo prevent freezing and possible bursting of pipes they should be\nwrapped, drained, or allowed to drip slowly.",
                "response": "Monitor",
                "parameters": {

                        "NPWBMX"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWUS74 KBMX 290524"
                    ],

                "description": "* WHAT...Blizzard conditions. Winds gusting as high as 50 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Point Lay\n\n* WHEN...Until 3 PM AKST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Visibilities may drop below 1/4 mile due to falling and\nblowing snow. Whiteout conditions are expected and will make\ntravel treacherous and potentially life-threatening. Areas of\nblowing snow could significantly reduce visibility.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Blizzard conditions are expected to persist\ntoward Point Lay through at least Thursday.",
                "instruction": "Travel should be restricted to emergencies only. If you must travel,\nhave a winter survival kit with you. If you get stranded, stay with\nyour vehicle. The latest road conditions for the state you are\ncalling from can be obtained by calling 5 1 1.",

                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 19 below.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of Eastern Upper and Northern Lower Michigan.\n\n* WHEN...Until 10 AM EST this morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 20 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 30 minutes.\nFrostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is\nexposed to these temperatures.                 "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 29 at 12:04AM EST until January 29 at 11:00AM EST by NWS Blacksburg VA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 14 below.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of northwest North Carolina, southwest Virginia,\nand southeast West Virginia.\n\n* WHEN...Until 11 AM EST this morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 14 below zero could\nresult in hypothermia if precautions are not taken.                 "headline": "Freeze Warning issued January 28 at 11:00PM CST until January 29 at 8:00AM CST by NWS New Orleans LA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Freeze Warning, sub-freezing temperatures as low as\n30 expected. For the Extreme Cold Watch, dangerously cold wind\nchills as low as 14 possible.\n\n* WHERE...Assumption, St. Charles, St. James, St. John The Baptist,\nUpper Lafourche, and Upper Terrebonne Parishes.\n\n* WHEN...For the Freeze Warning, until 8 AM CST Thursday. For the\nExtreme Cold Watch, from late Friday night through Sunday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...For the Freeze Warning: Frost and freeze conditions\ncould kill crops, other sensitive vegetation and possibly damage\nunprotected outdoor plumbing.\nFor the Extreme Cold Watch: Frostbite and hypothermia will occur\nif unprotected skin is exposed to these temperatures. An extended\nperiod of freezing temperatures could cause ruptured water pipes.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nTake steps now to protect tender plants from the cold.\n\nTo prevent water pipes from freezing; wrap or drain or allow them to\ndrip slowly.",

                "headline": "Extreme Cold Watch issued January 28 at 11:00PM CST until February 1 at 10:00AM CST by NWS New Orleans LA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Freeze Warning, sub-freezing temperatures as low as\n30 expected. For the Extreme Cold Watch, dangerously cold wind\nchills as low as 14 possible.\n\n* WHERE...Assumption, St. Charles, St. James, St. John The Baptist,\nUpper Lafourche, and Upper Terrebonne Parishes.\n\n* WHEN...For the Freeze Warning, until 8 AM CST Thursday. For the\nExtreme Cold Watch, from late Friday night through Sunday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...For the Freeze Warning: Frost and freeze conditions\ncould kill crops, other sensitive vegetation and possibly damage\nunprotected outdoor plumbing.\nFor the Extreme Cold Watch: Frostbite and hypothermia will occur\nif unprotected skin is exposed to these temperatures. An extended\nperiod of freezing temperatures could cause ruptured water pipes.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nTake steps now to protect tender plants from the cold.\n\nTo prevent water pipes from freezing; wrap or drain or allow them to\ndrip slowly.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the first Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 10 to 15 below zero.                 "senderName": "NWS Pittsburgh PA",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 28 at 11:48PM EST until January 30 at 11:00AM EST by NWS Pittsburgh PA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the first Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 10 to 15 below zero. For the second Cold Weather\nAdvisory, very cold temperatures as low as 5 to 15 below zero.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of east central Ohio, northwest, southwest, and\nwestern Pennsylvania, and northern and the northern panhandle of\nWest Virginia.\n\n* WHEN...For the first Cold Weather Advisory, until 11 AM EST\nThursday. For the second Cold Weather Advisory, from 7 PM Thursday\nto 11 AM EST Friday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 15 below zero could result\nin hypothermia or frostbite if precautions are not taken. Frostbite\nand hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is exposed to these\ntemperatures. An extended period of freezing temperatures could\ncause ruptured water pipes.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {

                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerously cold wind chills as low as 25 below zero\nexpected this morning.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 10 to 20\nkt with gusts up to 25 kt and choppy waters expected.                 "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for ice accumulation on their vessel and\nconsider altering plans to avoid or mitigate these hazardous\nconditions.\n\nInexperienced mariners, especially those operating smaller\nvessels, should avoid navigating in hazardous conditions.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 15 to 20\nkt with gusts up to 25 kt expected. For the Freezing Spray\nAdvisory, moderate accumulation of freezing spray expected.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal waters from Little Egg Inlet to Great Egg\nInlet NJ out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Great Egg Inlet to\nCape May NJ out 20 nm, Coastal waters from Cape May NJ to Cape\nHenlopen DE out 20 nm and Coastal waters from Cape Henlopen to\nFenwick Island DE out 20 nm.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 AM EST Friday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in freezing spray is hazardous.\nFreezing spray may render mechanical and electronic components\ninoperative.                 "senderName": "NWS Baltimore MD/Washington DC",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 28 at 11:08PM EST until January 29 at 11:00AM EST by NWS Baltimore MD/Washington DC",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 15 below zero.\n\n* WHERE...Augusta and Rockingham Counties.\n\n* WHEN...Until 11 AM EST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 15 below zero could\nresult in hypothermia or frostbite if precautions are not taken.\nFrostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is\nexposed to these temperatures. Wind chill values can lead to\nhypothermia with prolonged exposure. Very cold temperatures can\nlead to hypothermia with prolonged exposure.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Additional Cold Weather Headlines are\nexpected at times through the end of the week as the region\nremains in an Arctic airmass.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake sure outdoor animals have a warm, dry shelter, food, and\nunfrozen water.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "NPWLWX"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [

                    ],

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Extreme Cold Warning, dangerously cold wind chills\nas low as 25 below zero. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold\nwind chills as low as 15 below zero expected.\n\n* WHERE...In Virginia, Western Highland County. In West Virginia,\nWestern Grant and Western Pendleton Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Extreme Cold Warning, until 11 AM EST Thursday. For\nthe Cold Weather Advisory, from 11 AM Thursday to 11 AM EST Friday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills could cause frostbite\non exposed skin in as little as 30 minutes. Frostbite and\nhypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is exposed to these\ntemperatures. An extended period of freezing temperatures could\ncause ruptured water pipes. Wind chill values can lead to\nhypothermia with prolonged exposure. Very cold temperatures can\nlead to hypothermia with prolonged exposure.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Additional Cold Weather Headlines are\nexpected at times through the end of the week as the region\nremains in an Arctic airmass.",
                "instruction": "Dress in layers including a hat, face mask, and gloves if you must\ngo outside.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake sure outdoor animals have a warm, dry shelter, food, and\nunfrozen water.\n\nUse caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.",

                "senderName": "NWS Baltimore MD/Washington DC",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 28 at 11:08PM EST until January 29 at 11:00AM EST by NWS Baltimore MD/Washington DC",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the first Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 9 below zero. For the second Cold Weather Advisory, very\ncold wind chills as low as 9 below zero expected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of DC, central, northeast, northern, and southern\nMaryland, and northern Virginia.\n\n* WHEN...For the first Cold Weather Advisory, until 11 AM EST\nThursday. For the second Cold Weather Advisory, from 7 PM Thursday\nto 11 AM EST Friday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 9 below zero could result\nin hypothermia if precautions are not taken. Frostbite and\nhypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is exposed to these\ntemperatures. Wind chill values can lead to hypothermia with\nprolonged exposure. Very cold temperatures can lead to hypothermia\nwith prolonged exposure.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Additional Cold Weather Headlines are\nexpected at times through the end of the week as the region\nremains in an Arctic airmass.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake sure outdoor animals have a warm, dry shelter, food, and\nunfrozen water.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the first Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 15 below zero. Very cold temperatures can lead to hypothermia\nwith prolonged exposure.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Additional Cold Weather Headlines are\nexpected at times through the end of the week as the region\nremains in an Arctic airmass.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside.                 "event": "Coastal Flood Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Francisco CA",
                "headline": "Coastal Flood Advisory issued January 28 at 8:07PM PST until February 1 at 12:00PM PST by NWS San Francisco CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Beach Hazards Statement, an increased risk of\nsneaker waves and strong rip currents with breaking waves of 14\nto 19 feet. For the Coastal Flood Advisory, up to 1.4 ft of\ninundation above ground level is possible in low-lying areas near\nshorelines and tidal waterways.\n\n* WHERE...San Francisco and Monterey Bay.\n\n* WHEN...For the Beach Hazards Statement, from 9 PM PST this\nevening through 9 AM PST Friday morning. For the Coastal Flood\nAdvisory, from 6 AM PST Thursday to noon PST Sunday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Flooding of lots, parks, and roads with only\nisolated road closures expected. Dangerous conditions are\nforecast along the shoreline. Hazards include sneaker waves,\nstrong rip currents, and large breaking waves.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...At the San Francisco tidal gauge, high tide\nis expected to be: 1.2 ft above normal (7.02 ft MLLW) at 7:34 AM\nThursday 1/29, 1.1 ft above normal (6.98 ft MLLW) at 8:34 AM\nFriday 1/30, 1.4 ft above normal (7.24 ft MLLW) at 9:30 AM\nSaturday 1/31, 1.3 ft above normal (7.16 ft MLLW) at 10:22 AM\nSunday 2/1. These predictions include up to 0.4 ft of storm\nsurge that will enhance the astronomical tide and flooding\nthreat. High tide timing varies up to 90 minutes earlier or\nlater along the Pacific Coast and through the San Francisco Bay,\nrespectively.",
                "instruction": "If travel is required, allow extra time as some roads may be\nclosed. Do not drive around barricades or through water of\nunknown depth. Take the necessary actions to protect flood-prone\nproperty.\n\nStay off of jetties, piers, rocks, and other waterside\ninfrastructure. Remain out of the water to avoid hazardous surf\nand NEVER turn your back on the ocean. Monitor local weather,\nsurf and tide forecasts at www.weather.gov/mtr.",

For the Coastal Flood\nAdvisory, from 6 AM PST Thursday to noon PST Sunday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Flooding of lots, parks, and roads with only\nisolated road closures expected. Dangerous conditions are\nforecast along the shoreline. Hazards include sneaker waves,\nstrong rip currents, and large breaking waves.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...At the San Francisco tidal gauge, high tide\nis expected to be: 1.2 ft above normal (7.02 ft MLLW) at 7:34 AM\nThursday 1/29, 1.1 ft above normal (6.98 ft MLLW) at 8:34 AM\nFriday 1/30, 1.4 ft above normal (7.24 ft MLLW) at 9:30 AM\nSaturday 1/31, 1.3 ft above normal (7.16 ft MLLW) at 10:22 AM\nSunday 2/1. These predictions include up to 0.4 ft of storm\nsurge that will enhance the astronomical tide and flooding\nthreat. High tide timing varies up to 90 minutes earlier or\nlater along the Pacific Coast and through the San Francisco Bay,\nrespectively.",
                "instruction": "If travel is required, allow extra time as some roads may be\nclosed. Do not drive around barricades or through water of\nunknown depth. Take the necessary actions to protect flood-prone\nproperty.\n\nStay off of jetties, piers, rocks, and other waterside\ninfrastructure. Remain out of the water to avoid hazardous surf\nand NEVER turn your back on the ocean. Monitor local weather,\nsurf and tide forecasts at www.weather.gov/mtr.",

                "event": "High Surf Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Tiyan GU",
                "headline": "High Surf Advisory issued January 29 at 1:50PM ChST until January 31 at 3:00AM ChST by NWS Tiyan GU",
                "description": "* WHAT...Large breaking waves of 7 to 9 feet.\n\n* WHERE...North facing reefs of Pohnpei. North and east facing reefs\nof Kosrae.\n\n* WHEN...Until 3 AM ChST Saturday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Dangerous swimming and surfing conditions and\nlocalized beach erosion.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A pulse of long-period northwest swell, in\naddition to already significant east-northeast trade swell, are now\nexpected to continue to bring hazardous surf to Pohnpei and Kosrae\nshores through at least late Friday night or early Saturday\nmorning. Both trade swell and northwest swell are expected to begin\ndiminishing thereafter, allowing surf to drop below hazardous\nlevels but continue to remain elevated for the rest of the week.",
                "instruction": "Inexperienced swimmers should remain out of the water due to\ndangerous surf conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {

                    ],

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 28 at 9:44PM CST until February 2 at 6:00AM CST by NWS Lake Charles LA",
                "description": "...The National Weather Service in Lake Charles LA has issued a\nFlood Warning for the following rivers in Louisiana...Texas...\n\nSabine River Near Deweyville\n\nAdditional information is available at www.weather.gov.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Sabine River near Deweyville.\n\n* WHEN...From late Friday night to Monday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 24.0 feet, Minor lowland flooding will occur.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 8:45 PM CST Wednesday the stage was 22.8 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise above flood stage\nearly Saturday morning to a crest of 24.1 feet early Saturday\nafternoon.                 "senderName": "NWS Memphis TN",
                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 28 at 7:55PM CST until January 29 at 6:00PM CST by NWS Memphis TN",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning is extended for the following rivers in\nMississippi...\n\nButtahatchie River near Aberdeen\n\nFor the Buttahatchie River...including Aberdeen...Minor flooding is\nforecast.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring and minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Buttahatchie River near Aberdeen.\n\n* WHEN...Until tomorrow evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 15.0 feet, Water is covering a portion of Air Base\nRoad located on the west bank of the river. Low-lying farmland is\nflooded.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 7:30 PM CST Wednesday the stage was 15.0 feet.\n- Recent Activity...The maximum river stage in the 24 hours\nending at 7:30 PM CST Wednesday was 16.3 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to fall below flood stage\nearly tomorrow afternoon and continue falling to 9.1 feet\nMonday evening.\n- Flood stage is 13.0 feet.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.\n\nAdditional information is available at weather.gov/memphis.\n\nThe next statement will be issued as needed.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "senderName": "NWS Chicago IL",
                "headline": "Flood Advisory issued January 28 at 7:48PM CST by NWS Chicago IL",
                "description": "...The Flood Advisory continues for the following rivers in\nIllinois...\n\nKankakee River at Momence affecting Kankakee County.\n\nFor the Kankakee River...including Momence...elevated river levels\nare forecast.\n\n* WHAT...Flooding caused by an ice jam continues.\n\n* WHERE...Kankakee River from IL/IN state line downstream to\nconfluence with Iroquois River at Aroma Park, including the\nMomence gauge.\n\n* WHEN...Until further notice.\n\n* IMPACTS...Localized flooding of wetlands, forest land,\nagricultural land, and roadways may occur along the river.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 6:45 PM CST Wednesday the stage was 4.6 feet. The\nindicated stage was effected by an ice jam downstream of the\ngauge.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to remain near to just below\nflood stage while the ice jam is in place, with local water\nlevel fluctuations possible.\n- Flood stage is 5.0 feet.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "An ice jam is affecting water levels in this area. River forecasts\nduring ice jams are less certain due to many complicated factors.\nSignificant river ice cover can cause large water level fluctuations\nand flooding with little advanced notice. River ice may also cause\ngauge observations to be inaccurate.\n\nAdditional information is available at www.weather.gov.\n\nThe next statement will be issued by Thursday afternoon.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "description": "* WHAT...Sneaker waves possible.\n\n* WHERE...North and Central Coast of Oregon, and South\nWashington Coast.\n\n* WHEN...From 10 PM PST this evening through Thursday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Waves can run up significantly farther on a beach\nthan normal, including over rocks and jetties. Sneaker waves\ncan suddenly knock people off of their feet and quickly pull\nthem into the frigid ocean which may lead to serious injury or\ndrowning.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A Beach Hazards Statement is issued when\nsneaker waves create potentially life-threatening conditions in\nthe surf zone. Caution should be used when in or near the water.\nAlways keep an eye on the waves, and be especially watchful of\nchildren. Those participating in razor clam digs should also\nexercise caution and stay observant of incoming waves.",
                "instruction": "Remain out of the water to avoid hazardous swimming conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 28 at 7:06PM CST until January 30 at 6:00AM CST by NWS Mobile AL",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning continues for the following river in Alabama...\n\nTombigbee River Near Leroy affecting Washington and Clarke\nCounties.\n\nFor the Tombigbee River...including Coffeeville Dam, Coffeeville Dam\n- upstream, Leroy...Minor flooding is forecast.\n\nAdditional information is available at www.weather.gov/mob. Click on\nthe Rivers and Lakes menu for forecasts and observations.\n\nThe next statement will be issued when updates occur.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Tombigbee River near Leroy.\n\n* WHEN...From Thursday afternoon to Friday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 24.0 feet, flooding of low lying farm and\npasturelands begins. Cattle in low lying areas should be moved to\nhigher ground. Water approaches low lying roads on west side of\nriver.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 6:00 PM CST Wednesday the stage was 22.9 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise to a crest of 24.0\nfeet early tomorrow afternoon. It will then fall below flood\nstage early Friday morning.\n- Flood stage is 24.0 feet.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 28 at 7:05PM CST until January 30 at 1:00PM CST by NWS Mobile AL",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning continues for the following river in Alabama...\n\nTombigbee River Near Coffeeville Dam affecting Washington,\nChoctaw and Clarke Counties.\n\nFor the Tombigbee River...including Coffeeville Dam, Coffeeville Dam\n- upstream, Leroy...Minor flooding is forecast.\n\nAdditional information is available at www.weather.gov/mob. Click on\nthe Rivers and Lakes menu for forecasts and observations.\n\nThe next statement will be issued when updates occur.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring and minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Tombigbee River near Coffeeville Dam.\n\n* WHEN...Until early Friday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 29.0 feet, pastureland becomes flooded. Livestock\nshould be moved to higher ground. Old Ferry Road near the river\nbegins to flood.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 6:30 PM CST Wednesday the stage was 29.2 feet.\n- Recent Activity...The maximum river stage in the 24 hours\nending at 6:30 PM CST Wednesday was 29.3 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise to a crest of 29.7\nfeet early tomorrow afternoon.                 "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 28 at 7:04PM CST by NWS Memphis TN",
                "description": "Impacts from recent winter weather and freezing fog will creating\nhazardous conditions across the majoriy of the Midsouth tonight.\n\nHAZARDOUS ROADWAYS AND BLACK ICE:\nRoads across the region remain covered in snow, sleet, and solid\nice. Travel on secondary roads is virtually impossible in many\nlocations.\n\nEven where main thoroughfares have been treated, motorists should\nexercise extreme caution. Any slush or partial melting that\noccurred during the day will rapidly refreeze as temperatures\ndrop below freezing at night. This will result in the formation\nof widespread \"black ice\" -a transparent coating of ice that is\nnearly invisible to drivers but extremely slippery.\n\nPLEASE AVOID ALL NON-ESSENTIAL TRAVEL. If you must drive, keep a\nflashlight, food, and water in your vehicle in case of an\nemergency.\n\nRECOMMENDED SAFETY ACTIONS:\n\nHEAT SAFETY: If your power is out, NEVER use gas grills, charcoal\ngrills, or camping stoves indoors. These produce deadly carbon\nmonoxide gas which you cannot smell or see. Generators should be\noperated outdoors only, at least 20 feet away from windows and\ndoors.\n\nCONSERVE HEAT: Keep doors and windows closed. Close curtains and\nblinds to trap heat inside. Stuff towels or rags in cracks under\ndoors. Close off unused rooms to avoid wasting heat.\n\nLAYER CLOTHING: Wear loose-fitting, lightweight, warm clothing in\nseveral layers rather than a single heavy layer. Wear a hat and\nmittens to prevent body heat loss.\n\nCHECK YOUR PIPES: In homes without heat, open cabinet doors\nbeneath kitchen and bathroom sinks to allow warmer air to\ncirculate around pipes. Allow faucets to drip to prevent freezing.\n\nLOOK OUT FOR OTHERS: Check on elderly neighbors, friends, and\nfamily to ensure they are safe and warm, especially if they live\nalone or have lost power.",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "SPSMEG"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWUS84 KMEG 290104"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "DANGEROUS TRAVEL CONDITIONS PERSIST ACROSS THE MID-SOUTH DUE TO ICE, FREEZING FOG AND SNOW... ...SUB-FREEZING TEMPERATURES AND POWER OUTAGES INCREASE COLD WEATHER RISKS"
                    ],
                    "BLOCKCHANNEL": [
                        "EAS",

                "event": "Flood Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Jackson MS",
                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 28 at 5:32PM CST until January 30 at 10:48AM CST by NWS Jackson MS",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning is extended for the following rivers in\nMississippi...\n\nBig Black River At West affecting Attala and Holmes Counties.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring and minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Big Black River at West.\n\n* WHEN...Until late Friday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 17 feet, Several thousand acres of agricultural and\nlowland become inundated.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 4:45 PM CST Wednesday the stage was 16.6 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise to a crest of 16.8\nfeet just after midnight tonight.\n- Flood stage is 15.0 feet.\n- Flood History...This crest compares to a previous crest of\n17.4 feet on 12/30/2012.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.\n\nAdditional information is available at www.water.noaa.gov/wfo/jan\n\nThe next statement will be issued late tonight at 400 AM CST.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "FLSJAN"
                    ],

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Seattle WA",
                "headline": "Flood Watch issued January 28 at 3:30PM PST until January 31 at 4:00AM PST by NWS Seattle WA",
                "description": "A series of weather systems will bring heavy rain focused on the\nOlympic Peninsula this week.\n\n* WHAT...Flooding caused by excessive rainfall continues to be\npossible.\n\n* WHERE...A portion of northwest Washington, including the following\ncounty, Mason.\n\n* WHEN...Through late Friday night.\n\n* IMPACTS...Excessive runoff may result in flooding of rivers,\ncreeks, streams, and other low-lying and flood-prone locations.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- Heavy rainfall in the Olympic Mountains will bring sharp\nrises on the Skokomish River this week.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "You should monitor later forecasts and be alert for possible Flood\nWarnings. Those living in areas prone to flooding should be prepared\nto take action should flooding develop.",

                "senderName": "NWS Anchorage AK",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 28 at 1:29PM AKST by NWS Anchorage AK",
                "description": "A wintry mix of light snow and freezing rain is possible through\nThursday morning with a light glaze of ice possible. Rain is\nexpected for east Turnagain Arm and Portage Valley to the coast.\nHowever, any rain that falls onto residual packed snow and ice\ncould make travel difficult. Expect conditions to be more dry\ntowards Cook Inlet as the rain moves west over the Chugach and\nKenai Mountains and precipitation starts to dry out. Patchy areas\nof light freezing rain and snow cannot be ruled out though. Snow\nlevels this afternoon will be around 1000 feet, increasing to\naround 2000 feet tonight. Multiple rounds of precipitation is\nexpected with one main wave early this afternoon, another later\ntonight into early Thursday morning, and scattered light showers\nin between. Even though conditions have overall improved since the\nbig snow yesterday, please continue to travel with caution by\nslowing down, allow extra time to reach your destination, and\ncheck travel reports.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Grand Rapids MI",
                "headline": "Flood Advisory issued January 28 at 4:22PM EST until January 29 at 7:00PM EST by NWS Grand Rapids MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...Flooding along the Muskegon River remains possible due to\nan ice jam.\n\n* WHERE...A portion of west central Michigan, including the\nfollowing county, Newaygo.\n\n* WHEN...Until 700 PM EST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Ice jams are notoriously unpredictable. Low-lying areas\nalong the Muskegon River that are prone to flooding may see\nrapidly changing water levels as ice shifts and adjusts.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 418 PM EST, an ice jam continues to be reported by local\nauthorities on the Muskegon river in southern Newaygo County.\nThe river gauge at Newaygo shows water levels remaining at\nminor flood stage as of Wednesday afternoon.\n- As the river freezes up, ice can restrict the flow of the\nriver and cause rises and falls in water level. While\nflooding associated with freeze up ice jams is typically\nminor, conditions can change rapidly and unpredictably.\n- Some locations that will experience flooding include...\nNewaygo.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Please report observed flooding to local emergency services or law\nenforcement and request they pass this information to the National\nWeather Service when you can do so safely.",

                "senderName": "NWS Melbourne FL",
                "headline": "Freeze Warning issued January 28 at 12:43PM EST until January 29 at 9:00AM EST by NWS Melbourne FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Freeze Warning, sub-freezing temperatures as low as\n30 expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 25 expected. Sub-freezing temperatures are most likely\nin rural areas.\n\n* WHERE...Inland Volusia, Northern Lake, Okeechobee, Orange,\nOsceola, Seminole, and Southern Lake.\n\n* WHEN...For the Freeze Warning, from midnight tonight to 9 AM EST\nThursday. For the Cold Weather Advisory, from 2 AM to 9 AM EST\nThursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Very cold temperatures can lead to hypothermia with\nprolonged exposure. Frost and freeze conditions could kill crops,\nother sensitive vegetation and possibly damage unprotected outdoor\nplumbing.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nTake steps now to protect tender plants from the cold. Bring pets\nand potted plants indoors. Check on elderly neighbors to make sure\ntheir furnace is working. To prevent freezing and possible bursting\nof outdoor water pipes, they can be wrapped, drained or allowed to\ndrip slowly.",
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 28 at 10:49AM EST until January 29 at 12:00PM EST by NWS Albany NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...Flooding caused by an ice jam continues.\n\n* WHERE...West Canada Creek near Newport.\n\n* WHEN...Until noon EST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Depending on ice behavior, any release of the ice jam or\nbuildup of additional ice could result in sudden rises of water,\nleading to a rapid flooding situation.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 1033 AM EST, emergency management and local law\nenforcement reported an ice jam near Bridge Street in the\nvillage of Newport, with water out of the banks of West\nCanada Creek in Newport and upstream of the village.\n- Emergency management and local law enforcement report that\nwater levels have been slowly receding, but continue to\nfluctuate with dam releases upstream. Therefore, the threat\nof flooding due to the ice jam flood will continue until\nwater levels recede further.\n- Some locations that will experience flooding include...\nNewport.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.\n\nPlease report flooding to the National Weather Service by email at\nALB.stormreport@noaa.gov... On Facebook at\nwww.facebook.com/nwsalbany or twitter @nwsalbany.",
